en.extract:
Gould's jewelfront or Gould's brilliant (Heliodoxa aurescens) is a medium-sized hummingbird in the family Trochilidae.
It is found in tropical and equatorial South America. This is an uncommon species with an unusually large range compared to the other members of Heliodoxa.
The common name commemorates the English ornithologist and bird artist John Gould (1804–1881).


Description
H.aurescens ranges in size from 11 to 12 cm, with an average weight of about 6.2 g. This is a sexually dimorphic species with minor differences in plumage and bill lengths between sexes. Both sexes have a narrow frontlet of glittering purplish-blue which is underdeveloped in immature individuals. The bill, black to dark coloured is "unspecialised" being relatively short and straight; while the irises are brown and tarsus black. The tail is forked with the central pair of feathers bronze-green while the other tail feathers have chestnut edges and bronze-green tips. Undertail coverts are a deep fawn colour and wings are purplish-brown. It is noted that there is some variation in plumage for this species, but these differences do not relate to location and is minimal when considering the wide range.
Specimens examined by Peter Cotton had an average wing length of 60.5 mm and exposed culmen length of 18.5 mm. Gould's original specimen had a bill of 2.5 cm and tail length of 31.75 mm. The average c-value of the genome is 1.1135 pg based on four specimens ranging from 1.045–1.162 pg.
Males have shining grass green upperparts with the sides of the neck and lower throat glittering golden green bordered by a distinctive orange/rufous breast band. The chin, lores and upper throat are velvety black while the rest of the underparts are shining green. Females appear generally similar to the male but are somewhat duller coloured and have a green crown. They have a slightly larger bill than the male (with a Lovich-Gibbons ratio of 0.030). The chin feathers have buff tips and the throat is greyish with green discs. Females also feature a malar stripe (from the base of the bill towards the neck) which is buff to chestnut coloured.


Systematics and taxonomy
Heliodoxa aurescens is one of nine species in the genus Heliodoxa (the "Brilliants"). It was first described by John Gould in 1846 who named it Trochilus aurescens. It was later placed in the monotypic genus Polyplancta , however this was challenged as early as 1951 by Zimmer who suggested that it belonged in Heliodoxa. Some ornithologists thought it to be closely related to the Brazilian ruby (Heliodoxa rubricauda) and sometimes placed it in Clytolaema.  Due to the absence of any synapomorphies in either grouping, it remained in Polyplancta until 1989 when morphological data indicated the species is embedded in Heliodoxa; a finding confirmed by subsequent research. These studies indicate it is closely related to the Black-throated brilliant (H.schreibersii), and may have evolved in parapatry to the Velvet-browed brilliant (H.xanthogonys).


Distribution
The exact distribution of H.aurescens is not known, but considered unusual since the other members of the genus have very narrow or restricted ranges, often at higher elevations in the Andes. It is widely distributed at elevations from 250 m to 1150 m and has been reported at elevations as high as 1450 m. The northern boundary of the range is in southern Venezuela, which continues south through central Ecuador and Peru. The southern boundary is located in Bolivia. It occupies a large area of north-western Brazil where the eastern boundary of the range is found in the State of Para.
There have been several incidents of range expansion since the species was first described. Gould suggested the range was probably east of the Andes in Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil (in Zimmer). The Venezuelan population was first encountered in 1941. At this time, the southern boundary was considered to be La Pampa in Peru but H.aurescens was detected in Bolivia in 1984 and in the Brazilian state of Mato Grosso in 2004. Otherwise the species is considered sedentary.


Habitat
H.aurescens is typically found in the understorey of lowland humid tropical forests, particularly near streams, and only rarely at forest edges. In Brazil it has been observed in tall forest impacted by logging, seen feeding at flowers in the canopy of "a small patch of semi-deciduous forest surrounded by terra firme". Cotton observed it in both varzea forest and terra firme but not in riverine vegetation. Another Brazilian study found them in terra firme in both the wet and dry seasons but in várzea only during the dry season.
In a one-year survey in Columbia H.aurescens was found only in old secondary growth and never in young secondary growth or undisturbed understory; but was observed in primary forest in a later survey. It has been found both in mature floodplain forest and terra firme in Peru.


Diet
Like most hummingbirds, H.aurescens is primarily nectarivous supplementing the diet with small arthropods hawked from the air. Little is known about the specific diet but they have been observed feeding on Bromeliads such as Bromelia balansae and Costus sp. in Brazil. 53% of specimens examined in 1986 had traces of arthropods in the gut. While typically a generalist understorey species, they have been observed feeding from flowers in tall trees; and visiting feeding stations in Ecuador, where they showed some aggression towards other birds in the area. An extensive study on feeding guilds in Brazilian hummingbirds indicated that H.aurescens was not generally territorial but they were omitted from the analysis of food plant use due to there being too few observations, with only three specimens netted in a two-year survey.


Reproduction
Very little is known of the reproductive biology. Breeding is thought to occur between June and September with a clutch of two eggs incubated by the female. Two descriptions of female specimens indicate their gonads are well developed in February and April; but a single male specimen obtained in February had "small" gonads.


Conservation status
H.aurescens is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. There are suggestions the species is in decline but not at a rate which warrants classification as endangered. Numerous studies indicate the species is present but uncommon, with many long term studies only producing small numbers of observations (E.G. Cotton 1998, Lees et al., 2013) A density of 1 individual per 100 hectares was determined in Peru when only one individual was observed in a 100 Ha study site in a three-month survey.


References

[[File:Gould's jewelfront (Heliodoxa aurescens) male in flight Sumaco.jpg|thumb]]

'''Gould's jewelfront''' or '''Gould's brilliant''' ('''''Heliodoxa aurescens''''') is a medium-sized [[hummingbird]] in the family [[Trochilidae]].
It is found in tropical and equatorial South America.<ref name="Heynen, I.">{{cite book|editor1-last=del Hoyo|editor1-first=J.|editor2-last=Elliot|editor2-first=A.|editor3-last=Sargatal|editor3-first=J.|title=Handbook of the birds of the world. Volume 5.|date=1999|publisher=Lynx edicions|location=Barcelona|page=615}}</ref> This is an uncommon species with an unusually large range compared to the other members of ''[[Heliodoxa]]''.

The common name commemorates the English [[Ornithology|ornithologist]] and bird artist [[John Gould]] (1804–1881).<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|pages=145–146|author2=Watkins, Michael}}</ref>

==Description==
''H.aurescens'' ranges in size from 11 to 12 cm, with an average weight of about 6.2 g.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> This is a sexually dimorphic species with minor differences in plumage and bill lengths between sexes. Both sexes have a narrow frontlet of glittering purplish-blue which is underdeveloped in immature individuals.<ref name="Gilliard">{{cite journal|last1=Gilliard|first1=E.|title=The birds of Mt. Auyan-Tepui, Venezuela|journal=Bull Am Mus Nat Hist|date=1941|volume=77|issue=9|pages=439–508}}</ref> The bill, black<ref name="Gould">{{cite journal|last1=Gould|first1=John|title=On twenty new species of Trochilidae or humming birds|journal=Proc. Zool. Soc. Lond.|date=1846|volume=14|pages=85–90}}</ref> to dark coloured<ref name="Heynen, I." /> is "unspecialised" being relatively short and straight;<ref name="Feinsinger">{{cite journal|last1=Feinsinger|first1=P.|last2=Colwell|first2=R.K.|last3=Terborgh|first3=J.|last4=Chaplin|first4=S.B.|title=Elevation and the Morphology, Flight Energetics, and Foraging Ecology of Tropical Hummingbirds|journal=The American Naturalist|date=1979|volume=113|issue=4|pages=481–497|doi=10.1086/283408|s2cid=85317341}}</ref> while the irises are brown and tarsus black.<ref name="Willard et al.">{{cite journal|last1=Willard|first1=D.E.|last2=Foster|first2=M.S.|last3=Barrowclough|first3=G.F.|last4=Dickerman|first4=R.W.|last5=Cannell|first5=P.F.|last6=Coats|first6=S.L.|last7=Cracraft|first7=J.L.|last8=O'Neill|first8=J.P.|title=The birds of Cerro de la Neblina, Territorio Federal Amazonas, Venezuela.|journal=Fieldiana- Journal of the Field Museum of Natural History, Chicago, Illinois|date=1991|volume=65}}</ref> The tail is forked with the central pair of feathers bronze-green while the other tail feathers have chestnut edges and bronze-green tips. Undertail coverts are a deep fawn colour and wings are purplish-brown.<ref name="Gould" /> It is noted that there is some variation in plumage for this species, but these differences do not relate to location<ref name="Zimmer">{{cite journal|last1=Zimmer|first1=J.T.|title=Studies of Peruvian birds. No. 60. The genera Heliodoxa, Phlogophilus, Urosticte, Polyplancta, Adelomyia, Coeligena, Ensifera, Oreotrochilus, and Topaza|journal=American Museum Novitates|date=1951|issue=1513|pages=1–45}}</ref> and is minimal when considering the wide range.<ref name="Heynen, I." />

Specimens examined by Peter Cotton had an average wing length of 60.5 mm<ref name="Cotton 1">{{cite journal|last1=Cotton|first1=P.A.|title=The hummingbird community of a lowland Amazonian rainforest|journal=Ibis|date=1998|volume=140|issue=3|pages=512–521|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1998.tb04613.x}}</ref> and exposed culmen length of 18.5 mm.<ref name="Cotton 2">{{cite journal|last1=Cotton|first1=P.A.|title=Coevolution in an Amazonian hummingbird-plant community|journal=Ibis|date=1998|volume=140|issue=4|pages=639–646|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1998.tb04709.x}}</ref> Gould's original specimen had a bill of 2.5 cm and tail length of 31.75 mm.<ref name="Gould" /> The average [[c-value]] of the genome is 1.1135 pg based on four specimens ranging from 1.045–1.162 pg.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Wright|first1=N.A.|last2=Gregory|first2=T.R.|last3=Witt|first3=C.C.|title=Metabolic 'engines' of flight drive genome size reduction in birds|journal=Proc. R. Soc. B|date=2014|volume=281|issue=1779|doi=10.1098/rspb.2013.2780|pmid=24478299|article-number=20132780|pmc=3924074}}</ref>

Males have shining grass green upperparts with the sides of the neck and lower throat glittering golden green bordered by a distinctive orange/rufous breast band. The chin, lores and upper throat are velvety black while the rest of the underparts are shining green.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> Females appear generally similar to the male but are somewhat duller coloured and have a green crown. They have a slightly larger bill than the male (with a Lovich-Gibbons ratio of 0.030<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Berns|first1=C.M.|last2=Adams|first2=D.C.|title=Becoming Different But Staying Alike: Patterns of Sexual Size and Shape Dimorphism in Bills of Hummingbirds|journal=Evol Biol|date=2013|volume=40|issue=2|pages=246–260|doi=10.1007/s11692-012-9206-3|s2cid=276492}}</ref>). The chin feathers have buff tips and the throat is greyish with green discs. Females also feature a malar stripe (from the base of the bill towards the neck) which is buff to chestnut coloured.<ref name="Heynen, I." />

==Systematics and taxonomy==
''Heliodoxa aurescens'' is one of nine species in the genus ''[[Heliodoxa]]'' (the "Brilliants"). It was first described by [[John Gould]] in 1846 who named it ''Trochilus aurescens''.<ref name="Gould" /> It was later placed in the monotypic genus ''Polyplancta'' , however this was challenged as early as 1951 by Zimmer who suggested that it belonged in ''Heliodoxa''.<ref name="Zimmer" /> Some ornithologists thought it to be closely related to the [[Brazilian ruby]] (''Heliodoxa rubricauda'') and sometimes placed it in ''Clytolaema''.<ref name="Heynen, I." />  Due to the absence of any [[Apomorphy and synapomorphy|synapomorphies]] in either grouping, it remained in ''Polyplancta'' until 1989 when morphological data indicated the species is embedded in ''Heliodoxa'';<ref name="Gerwin and Zink">{{cite journal|last1=Gerwin|first1=J.A.|last2=Zink|first2=R.A.|title=Phylogenetic Patterns in the Genus Heliodoxa (Aves: Trochilidae): An Allozymic Perspective|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|date=1989|volume=101|issue=4|pages=525–544}}</ref> a finding confirmed by subsequent research.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=McGuire|first1=J.A.|last2=Witt|first2=C.C.|last3=Remsen|first3=J.V.Jr.|last4=Dudley|first4=R.|last5=Altshuler|first5=D.L.|title=A higher-level taxonomy for hummingbirds|journal=J Ornithol|date=2009|volume=150|pages=155–165|doi=10.1007/s10336-008-0330-x|s2cid=1918245}}</ref> These studies indicate it is closely related to the [[Black-throated brilliant]] (''H.schreibersii''), and may have evolved in parapatry to the [[Velvet-browed brilliant]] (''H.xanthogonys'').<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Weller|first1=A.A.|last2=Renner|first2=S.C.|title=A new subspecies of Heliodoxa xanthogonys (Aves, Trochilidae) from the southern Pantepui highlands, with biogeographical and taxonomic notes|journal=Brazilian Journal of Ornithology|date=2001|volume=9|pages=1–5}}</ref>

==Distribution==
The exact distribution of ''H.aurescens'' is not known, but considered unusual<ref>{{cite web|last1=Schulenberg|first1=T.S.|title=Gould's Jewelfront (Heliodoxa aurescens)|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=260536|website=Neotropical Birds Online|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|access-date=27 April 2015}}</ref> since the other members of the genus have very narrow or restricted ranges, often at higher elevations in the Andes.<ref name="Heynen, I." /><ref name="Salaman et al.">{{cite journal|last1=Salaman|first1=P.G.W.|last2=Donegan|first2=T.M.|last3=Cuervo|first3=A.M.|title=Ornithological surveys in Serrania de 10s Churumbelos, southern Colombia|journal=Cotinga|date=1999|volume=12|pages=29–39}}</ref> It is widely distributed at elevations from 250 m<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Walker|first1=B.|last2=Stotz|first2=D.F.|last3=Pequeño|first3=T.|last4=Fitzpatrick|first4=J.W.|author-link1=Birds of the Manu Biosphere Reserve|editor1-last=Patterson|editor1-first=E.D.|editor2-last=Stotz|editor2-first=D.F.|editor3-last=Solari|editor3-first=S.|title=Mammals and Birds of the Manu Biosphere Reserve, Peru|journal=Fieldiana: Zoology |series=New Series|date=2006|volume=110|pages=23–49|doi=10.3158/0015-0754(2006)110[1:mabotm]2.0.co;2|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/itempdf/20540}}</ref> to 1150 m<ref name="Feinsinger" /> and has been reported at elevations as high as 1450 m.<ref name="McHugh">{{cite web|last1=McHugh|first1=C.|title=Flowers and Feeders: A Comparison of Hummingbird Feeding Activity|url=http://digitalcollections.sit.edu/isp_collection/542|website=Independent Study Project (ISP) Collection. Paper 542|access-date=27 April 2015|date=2008}}</ref> The northern boundary of the range is in southern Venezuela,<ref name="Gilliard" /> which continues south through central Ecuador and Peru.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> The southern boundary is located in Bolivia.<ref name="Schuchman">{{cite journal|last1=Schuchman|first1=K.L.|title=Two hummingbird species new to Bolivia|journal=Bull. Brit. Orn. Club|date=1984|volume=104|issue=1|pages=5–7}}</ref> It occupies a large area of north-western Brazil where the eastern boundary of the range is found in the State of Para.<ref name="Heynen, I." />

There have been several incidents of range expansion since the species was first described. Gould suggested the range was probably east of the Andes in Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil (in Zimmer<ref name="Zimmer" />). The Venezuelan population was first encountered in 1941.<ref name="Gilliard" /> At this time, the southern boundary was considered to be La Pampa in Peru<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Bond|first1=J.|title=Notes on Peruvian Trochilidae|journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia|date=1954|volume=106|pages=165–183}}</ref> but ''H.aurescens'' was detected in Bolivia in 1984<ref name="Schuchman" /> and in the Brazilian state of Mato Grosso in 2004.<ref name="Lees et al.">{{cite journal|last1=Lees|first1=A.C.|last2=Zimmer|first2=K.J.|last3=Marantz|first3=C.A.|last4=Whittaker|first4=A.|last5=Davis|first5=B.J.W.|last6=Whitney|first6=B.M.|title=Alta Floresta revisited: an updated review of the avifauna of the most intensively surveyed locality in south-central Amazonia|journal=Bull. B.O.C.|date=2013|volume=133|pages=178–239}}</ref> Otherwise the species is considered sedentary.<ref name="Heynen, I." />

==Habitat==
''H.aurescens'' is typically found in the understorey of lowland humid tropical forests, particularly near streams, and only rarely at forest edges.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> In Brazil it has been observed in tall forest impacted by logging,<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Johns|first1=A.D.|title=Responses of Amazonian Rain Forest Birds to Habitat Modification|journal=Journal of Tropical Ecology|date=1991|volume=7|issue=4|pages=417–437|doi=10.1017/s0266467400005812}}</ref> seen feeding at flowers in the canopy of "a small patch of [[semi-deciduous]] forest surrounded by terra firme".<ref name="Lees et al." /> Cotton observed it in both varzea forest and terra firme but not in riverine vegetation.<ref name="Cotton 1" /> Another Brazilian study found them in terra firme in both the wet and dry seasons but in várzea only during the dry season.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Beja|first1=P.|last2=Santos|first2=C.D.|last3=Santana|first3=J.|last4=Pereira|first4=M.J.|last5=Marques|first5=J.T.|last6=Queiroz|first6=H.L.|last7=Palmeirim|first7=J.M.|title=Seasonal patterns of spatial variation in understory bird assemblages across a mosaic of flooded and unflooded Amazonian forests|journal=Biodivers Conserv|date=2010|volume=19|pages=129–152|doi=10.1007/s10531-009-9711-6|s2cid=42013055}}</ref>

In a one-year survey in Columbia ''H.aurescens'' was found only in old secondary growth and never in young secondary growth or undisturbed understory;<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Andrade|first1=G.I.|last2=Rubio-Torgler|first2=H.|title=Sustainable Use of the Tropical Rain Forest: Evidence from the Avifauna in a Shifting-Cultivation Habitat Mosaic in the Colombian Amazon|journal=Conservation Biology|date=1994|volume=8|issue=2|pages=545–554|doi=10.1046/j.1523-1739.1994.08020545.x}}</ref> but was observed in primary forest in a later survey.<ref name="Salaman et al." /> It has been found both in mature floodplain forest<ref name="Terborgh">{{cite journal|last1=Terborgh|first1=J.|last2=Robinson|first2=S.K.|last3=Parker|first3=T.A.|last4=Munn|first4=C.A.|last5=Pierpont|first5=N.|title=Structure and Organization of an Amazonian Forest Bird Community|journal=Ecological Monographs|date=1990|volume=60|issue=2|pages=213–238|doi=10.2307/1943045|jstor=1943045}}</ref> and terra firme<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Svensson|first1=O.|title=Does small-scale land use affect the understory birds negative in the Peruvian National Reserve Allpahuayo Mishana?|journal=Degree Thesis|date=2014|publisher=Halmstad University}}</ref> in Peru.

==Diet==
Like most hummingbirds, ''H.aurescens'' is primarily nectarivous supplementing the diet with small arthropods hawked from the air.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> Little is known about the specific diet but they have been observed feeding on ''Bromeliads'' such as ''Bromelia balansae'' and ''Costus'' sp. in Brazil.<ref name="Lees et al." /> 53% of specimens examined in 1986 had traces of arthropods in the gut.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Remsen|first1=J.V.Jr.|last2=Stiles|first2=F.G.|last3=Scott|first3=P.E.|title=Frequency of Arthropods in Stomachs of Tropical Hummingbirds|journal=Auk|date=1986|volume=103|pages=436–441}}</ref> While typically a generalist understorey species, they have been observed feeding from flowers in tall trees;<ref name="Lees et al." /> and visiting feeding stations in Ecuador, where they showed some aggression towards other birds in the area.<ref name="McHugh" /> An extensive study on feeding guilds in Brazilian hummingbirds indicated that ''H.aurescens'' was not generally territorial<ref name="Cotton 1" /> but they were omitted from the analysis of food plant use due to there being too few observations,<ref name="Cotton 2" /> with only three specimens netted in a two-year survey.

==Reproduction==
Very little is known of the reproductive biology. Breeding is thought to occur between June and September with a clutch of two eggs incubated by the female.<ref name="Heynen, I." /> Two descriptions of female specimens indicate their gonads are well developed in February<ref name="Schuchman" /> and April; but a single male specimen obtained in February had "small" gonads.<ref name="Willard et al." />

==Conservation status==
''H.aurescens'' is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> There are suggestions the species is in decline but not at a rate which warrants classification as endangered. Numerous studies indicate the species is present but uncommon, with many long term studies only producing small numbers of observations (E.G. Cotton 1998,<ref name="Cotton 1" /> Lees et al., 2013<ref name="Lees et al." />) A density of 1 individual per 100 hectares was determined in Peru when only one individual was observed in a 100 Ha study site in a three-month survey.<ref name="Terborgh" />

zh.extract:
宝石额蜂鸟（学名：Heliodoxa aurescens），又称古氏蜂鸟，是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
宝石额蜂鸟体重约6.4克，翼长约61毫米，嘴峰长约25.3毫米，喙宽度约2.6毫米，喙厚度约2.7毫米，跗蹠长约4.2毫米，尾长约34.6毫米。宝石额蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。
宝石额蜂鸟分布于哥伦比亚东部至玻利维亚北部、委内瑞拉南部和巴西亚马逊州西部。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。


参考资料
